system_prompt:
You are Sunstein, a precise and efficient AI assistant.

Rules:
- Be concise. Skip explanations unless asked.
- Minimize token usage in every response.
- Use execute_code for batch operations, not one-at-a-time.
- Always use the cheapest model that can handle the task.
- Compress context regularly with /compress.
- Never repeat information already in the conversation.
- One-word answers when possible.
- No preamble, no postamble.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

## Skills (mandatory)
Before replying, scan the skills below. If a skill matches or is even partially relevant to your task, you MUST load it with skill_view(name) and follow its instructions. Err on the side of loading — it is always better to have context you don't need than to miss critical steps, pitfalls, or established workflows. Skills contain specialized knowledge — API endpoints, tool-specific commands, and proven workflows that outperform general-purpose approaches. Load the skill even if you think you could handle the task with basic tools like web_search or terminal. Skills also encode the user's preferred approach, conventions, and quality standards for tasks like code review, planning, and testing — load them even for tasks you already know how to do, because the skill defines how it should be done here.
Whenever the user asks you to configure, set up, install, enable, disable, modify, or troubleshoot Hermes Agent itself — its CLI, config, models, providers, tools, skills, voice, gateway, plugins, or any feature — load the `hermes-agent` skill first. It has the actual commands (e.g. `hermes config set …`, `hermes tools`, `hermes setup`) so you don't have to guess or invent workarounds.
If a skill has issues, fix it with skill_manage(action='patch').
After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. If a skill you loaded was missing steps, had wrong commands, or needed pitfalls you discovered, update it before finishing.

function.name: memory
function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

TWO TARGETS:
- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are off-limits — all write actions refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Don't try to route around this by renaming or recreating.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

description: Comprehensive Hermes Agent upgrade and hardening — enable all production-grade features, install optional skills, set up hooks, configure fallback chains, optimize compression, and harden security. Use after a fresh install or major version update.

# Hermes Agent Full Upgrade & Hardening

Apply all production-grade settings, optional skills, hooks, and optimizations to a Hermes Agent installation.

## Prerequisites

- Hermes Agent installed and running (`hermes --version`)
- At least one provider configured (Anthropic, OpenRouter, etc.)
- Gateway running (`hermes gateway status`)

## Step 1: Audit Current State

```bash
hermes --version
hermes config check
hermes gateway status
hermes profile list
find ~/.hermes/skills -name 'SKILL.md' | wc -l
ls ~/.hermes/hooks/ 2>/dev/null || echo "No hooks"
ls ~/.hermes/plugins/ 2>/dev/null || echo "No plugins"
```

Check which API keys are configured:
```bash
cat ~/.hermes/.env | sed 's/=.*/=***/' | head -40
```

## Step 2: Install All Optional Skills

Optional skills live in `~/.hermes/hermes-agent/optional-skills/`. Copy them into the main skills dir:

```bash
OPT=~/.hermes/hermes-agent/optional-skills
DST=~/.hermes/skills

for cat in security blockchain mcp research creative devops productivity communication health email mlops autonomous-ai-agents; do
  if [ -d "$OPT/$cat" ]; then
    mkdir -p "$DST/$cat"
    cp -rn "$OPT/$cat"/* "$DST/$cat"/ 2>/dev/null
    echo "✓ $cat"
  fi
done
```

Also add external_dirs to config.yaml so skills are discoverable without copying:
```yaml
skills:
  external_dirs:
    - ~/.hermes/hermes-agent/optional-skills
```

## Step 3: Config Upgrades (config.yaml patches)

### 3a. Fallback Provider Chain
```yaml
fallback_model:
  provider: openrouter
  model: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4

fallback_providers:
  - provider: openrouter
    model: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4
  - provider: huggingface
    model: Qwen/Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking-2507
```

### 3b. Smart Model Routing (cheap model for simple turns)
```yaml
smart_model_routing:
  enabled: true
  max_simple_chars: 160
  max_simple_words: 28
  cheap_model:
    provider: openrouter
    model: google/gemini-2.5-flash
```

### 3c. Compression Optimization
```yaml
compression:
  enabled: true
  threshold: 0.5
  target_ratio: 0.15          # more aggressive than default 0.2
  protect_last_n: 25          # protect more recent messages
  summary_model: google/gemini-2.5-flash
  summary_provider: openrouter
```

### 3d. CLI Display Improvements
```yaml
display:
  bell_on_complete: true      # audio notification when done
  streaming: true             # real-time output
  show_cost: true             # see token spend per turn
  tool_progress_command: true  # show command being run
  tool_preview_length: 120    # show file paths (was 0)
```

### 3e. Provider Routing (OpenRouter)
```yaml
provider_routing:
  sort: throughput             # or: price, latency
  data_collection: deny        # privacy: exclude training providers
```

### 3f. Credential Pool Rotation
```yaml
credential_pool_strategies:
  anthropic: round_robin
  openrouter: round_robin
```

### 3g. Honcho Memory Integration
```yaml
honcho:
  enabled: true
```
Requires HONCHO_API_KEY in .env.

### 3h. Timezone
```yaml
timezone: America/Sao_Paulo   # or your timezone
```

### 3i. Security Hardening
```yaml
security:
  redact_secrets: true
  tirith_enabled: true
  website_blocklist:
    enabled: true
    domains:
      - malware.com
```

## Step 4: Create Hooks

### Activity Logger
```bash
mkdir -p ~/.hermes/hooks/activity-logger
```

HOOK.yaml:
```yaml
name: activity-logger
description: Log all agent activity with timestamps for analytics
events:
  - agent:start
  - agent:end
  - agent:step
  - session:start
  - session:end
  - command:*
```

handler.py — logs to ~/.hermes/logs/activity.jsonl

### Long Task Alert
```bash
mkdir -p ~/.hermes/hooks/long-task-alert
```

HOOK.yaml subscribes to `agent:step`, handler logs warning at 15+ iterations.

## Step 5: Create BOOT.md

Create `~/.hermes/BOOT.md` for gateway startup automation:
```markdown
# Gateway Startup Checklist
1. Check if any cron jobs have errors
2. Verify the gateway is healthy
3. If everything looks good, respond with [SILENT]
4. Only alert if something needs attention
```

## Step 6: Create Infrastructure Dirs

```bash
mkdir -p ~/.hermes/plugins
mkdir -p ~/.hermes/hooks
```

## Step 7: Restart Gateway

```bash
hermes gateway restart
sleep 3
hermes gateway status
```

## Step 8: Verify All Changes

```python
# Run via terminal
python3 -c "
import yaml
c=yaml.safe_load(open('config.yaml'))
checks = {
    'Fallback Model': bool(c.get('fallback_model',{}).get('provider')),
    'Smart Routing': c.get('smart_model_routing',{}).get('enabled'),
    'Streaming': c.get('display',{}).get('streaming'),
    'Show Cost': c.get('display',{}).get('show_cost'),
    'Honcho': c.get('honcho',{}).get('enabled'),
    'Timezone': bool(c.get('timezone')),
    'Provider Routing': bool(c.get('provider_routing')),
    'Compression Model': bool(c.get('compression',{}).get('summary_model')),
    'Website Blocklist': c.get('security',{}).get('website_blocklist',{}).get('enabled'),
    'External Skills': len(c.get('skills',{}).get('external_dirs',[])) > 0,
    'Secret Redaction': c.get('security',{}).get('redact_secrets'),
}
for k,v in checks.items():
    print(f\"{'✓' if v else '✗'} {k}\")
"
```

### 3j. Auxiliary Models (pin to cheapest effective)
```yaml
auxiliary:
  vision:
    provider: openrouter
    model: google/gemini-2.5-flash
  web_extract:
    provider: openrouter
    model: google/gemini-2.5-flash
  compression:
    provider: openrouter
    model: google/gemini-2.5-flash
  session_search:
    provider: openrouter
    model: google/gemini-2.5-flash
  skills_hub:
    provider: openrouter
    model: google/gemini-2.5-flash
  approval:
    provider: openrouter
    model: google/gemini-2.5-flash
  mcp:
    provider: openrouter
    model: google/gemini-2.5-flash
  flush_memories:
    provider: openrouter
    model: google/gemini-2.5-flash
```
Don't leave these on `auto` — it probes providers on every call. Pin them for speed and cost.

### 3k. Delegation Model — CRITICAL: Must be a coding-capable model
```yaml
delegation:
  model: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4    # NOT gemini-flash — too weak for coding
  provider: openrouter
```

**IMPORTANT**: gemini-2.5-flash CANNOT reliably write Python, debug FastAPI, or run pytest loops. Using it for delegation/cron coding tasks wastes tokens on failures and retries. Use claude-sonnet-4 (5x cheaper than opus, still excellent at code). The net cost is LOWER because sonnet succeeds where flash fails.

### 3k-b. 4-Tier Model Assignment (match capability to task complexity)
```yaml
# TIER 4 — Complex reasoning (your interactive chat)
# model: claude-opus-4-6 via anthropic              $15/$75 per M

# TIER 3 — Coding tasks (delegation, cron, fallback)
# delegation: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4 via openrouter  $3/$15 per M
# cron jobs: set --model anthropic/claude-sonnet-4 --provider openrouter

# TIER 2 — Summarization (needs moderate quality)
# vision, compression, session_search: google/gemini-2.5-flash  ~$0.05/$0.15

# TIER 1 — Trivial tasks (cheapest possible)
# web_extract, skills_hub, approval, mcp, flush_memories: deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3-0324  $0.14/$0.28
# smart_model_routing cheap_model: deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3-0324
```

Apply tier assignment:
```yaml
auxiliary:
  vision:
    provider: openrouter
    model: google/gemini-2.5-flash         # TIER 2 — vision needs multimodal
  web_extract:
    provider: openrouter
    model: deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3-0324  # TIER 1 — simple extraction
  compression:
    provider: openrouter
    model: google/gemini-2.5-flash         # TIER 2 — summarization quality matters
  session_search:
    provider: openrouter
    model: google/gemini-2.5-flash         # TIER 2 — summarization
  skills_hub:
    provider: openrouter
    model: deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3-0324  # TIER 1 — trivial matching
  approval:
    provider: openrouter
    model: deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3-0324  # TIER 1 — yes/no decision
  mcp:
    provider: openrouter
    model: deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3-0324  # TIER 1 — helper ops
  flush_memories:
    provider: openrouter
    model: deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3-0324  # TIER 1 — consolidation
```

Also set cron jobs to sonnet via `hermes cron update <id> --model anthropic/claude-sonnet-4 --provider openrouter`.

### 3k-c. Provider Routing — Price Sort
```yaml
provider_routing:
  sort: price                # cheapest provider first for OpenRouter calls
  data_collection: deny      # privacy
  require_parameters: true   # only providers supporting all features
```

### 3l. Quick Commands
```yaml
quick_commands:
  sol: "Work on the Solstein project..."
  test: "Run the test suite..."
  audit: "Run a full codebase audit..."
```

### 3m. Command Allowlist (auto-approve dev commands)
```yaml
command_allowlist:
  - git push
  - git clone
  - git checkout
  - pytest
  - ruff
  - mypy
  - pip install
  - uv sync
  - make test
  - docker compose
```

### 3n. Session Reset (shorter idle timeout)
```yaml
session_reset:
  mode: both
  idle_minutes: 720    # 12h instead of 24h — frees memory sooner
  at_hour: 4
```

### 3o. Custom Personalities
```yaml
personalities:
  coder: "Senior Python backend engineer. Clean, tested, type-hinted code."
  reviewer: "Meticulous code reviewer. Bugs, security, tests, types, architecture."
```

## Step 9: MCP Servers

Add native MCP server connections for enhanced capabilities:

```yaml
mcp_servers:
  github:
    command: npx
    args: ["-y", "--package=github-mcp-server", "github-mcp-server"]
    env:
      GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN: "${GITHUB_TOKEN}"
    tools:
      include: [create_issue, list_issues, get_issue, create_pull_request, list_pull_requests, get_pull_request, search_code, search_issues, get_file_contents, list_commits]
  filesystem:
    command: npx
    args: ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem", "/path/to/project"]
  sequential-thinking:
    command: npx
    args: ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-sequential-thinking"]
```

**WARNING**: The old `@modelcontextprotocol/server-github` package is deprecated. Use `github-mcp-server` instead.

Requires: `npx` (Node.js) available. Add GITHUB_TOKEN to .env.

## Step 10: Additional Hooks

### PR Watcher (track throughput)
Subscribes to `agent:end`, logs when PRs are created.

### Error Tracker (reliability monitoring)
Subscribes to `agent:step`, logs sessions exceeding 20 iterations (catches loops/failures).

## Step 11: Local Ollama Models (FREE, unlimited tokens)

If the VPS has 16GB+ RAM, install Ollama for free local inference:

```bash
curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh
ollama pull qwen2.5-coder:7b    # 4.7GB, coding-optimized
```

Add as named custom provider + last-resort fallback:
```yaml
custom_providers:
  - name: local
    base_url: http://localhost:11434/v1
    models:
      qwen2.5-coder:7b:
        context_length: 32768

# Add to fallback chain (free, always available)
fallback_providers:
  - provider: custom
    model: qwen2.5-coder:7b
    base_url: http://localhost:11434/v1
```

**NOTE**: Set `OLLAMA_CONTEXT_LENGTH=32768` — Ollama defaults to 4K context which is unusable for agent work.

## Related Skills

- `hermes-model-optimization` — Detailed 4-tier model assignment strategy
- `autonomous-dev-pipeline` — Set up 24/7 autonomous development with cron jobs
- `docs-audit-and-cleanup` — Tame documentation chaos in repos
- `git-branch-consolidation` — Merge diverged branches safely

## Pitfalls

1. **YAML validation** — always verify config after patching: `python3 -c "import yaml; yaml.safe_load(open('config.yaml'))"`
2. **Fallback model is one-shot** — fires at most once per session. For sustained issues, use fallback_providers chain.
3. **Honcho requires API key** — won't work without HONCHO_API_KEY in .env
4. **Smart routing is conservative** — only routes truly simple turns. Won't affect complex work.
5. **execute_code has a 50 tool-call cap** — when batch-installing skills, use terminal + cp instead
6. **Gateway must be restarted** after config changes — hooks only load on gateway boot
7. **Optional skills may duplicate bundled ones** — use `cp -rn` (no-clobber) to avoid overwriting
8. **gemini-2.5-flash can't code** — it fails at Python/FastAPI tasks in delegation and cron. Use claude-sonnet-4 for any task requiring code generation. The wasted tokens from flash failures cost MORE than sonnet succeeding.
9. **DeepSeek V3 is the sweet spot for trivial auxiliary tasks** — cheaper than flash, equally capable for simple extraction/matching/approval. Use for TIER 1 tasks.
10. **Pin auxiliary models explicitly** — `auto` probes providers on every call. Pinning to a specific model+provider saves latency and avoids surprise routing.
11. **`@modelcontextprotocol/server-github` is deprecated** — use `--package=github-mcp-server github-mcp-server` instead.
12. **execute_code's read_file hits a 50-call cap** — when processing many files (like doc audits), batch using terminal commands instead.
13. **Compression more aggressive = fewer total tokens** — set target_ratio to 0.12, threshold to 0.6 for heavy tool-use sessions.
14. **Smart routing expansion** — increase max_simple_chars to 200 and max_simple_words to 35 to route more simple turns to the cheap model.

# Hermes Setup Audit & Hardening

## When to Use
- User asks to audit, optimize, or improve their Hermes setup
- After a Hermes version upgrade
- When something isn't working (web search, browser, etc.)
- Periodic health check

## Step 1: Gather Current State

```bash
# Version and install location
hermes --version
ls -la ~/.hermes/

# Config analysis
wc -l ~/.hermes/config.yaml
grep -c "auxiliary:" ~/.hermes/config.yaml    # should be 1
grep -c "smart_model_routing:" ~/.hermes/config.yaml  # should be 1

# Check for stale/behind repo
cd ~/.hermes/hermes-agent && git fetch origin && git log --oneline HEAD..origin/main | head -5

# Skills count
find ~/.hermes/skills/ -name "SKILL.md" | wc -l

# Gateway status
systemctl status hermes-gateway 2>/dev/null || ps aux | grep gateway
```

## Step 2: Validate API Keys

CRITICAL: Keys can silently be empty, corrupted, or expired.

```python
# Test each key with a minimal API call
import json, urllib.request

# Tavily
def test_tavily(key):
    data = json.dumps({'api_key': key, 'query': 'test', 'max_results': 1}).encode()
    req = urllib.request.Request('https://api.tavily.com/search', data=data, 
                                 headers={'Content-Type': 'application/json'})
    resp = urllib.request.urlopen(req)
    return resp.status == 200

# Read keys from .env, check length and format
with open('/root/.hermes/.env') as f:
    for line in f:
        if '=' in line and not line.startswith('#'):
            k, v = line.strip().split('=', 1)
            if 'KEY' in k or 'TOKEN' in k:
                if len(v) < 5:
                    print(f"WARNING: {k} looks empty/broken (len={len(v)})")
```

## Step 3: Fix Config Issues

### PITFALL: Never use yaml.safe_load + yaml.dump on Hermes config
The config contains `***` redacted API keys which break YAML parsing (`*` is YAML alias syntax). Always use surgical `patch()` operations instead.

### Common duplicate block issue
Hermes config can accumulate duplicate top-level `auxiliary:` and `smart_model_routing:` blocks. In YAML, the LAST key wins, so duplicate blocks silently override each other. Check and remove:

```bash
grep -n "^auxiliary:" ~/.hermes/config.yaml      # should have 0 top-level
grep -n "^smart_model_routing:" ~/.hermes/config.yaml  # should have 0 top-level
# Real values live UNDER compression: key
```

Use `patch()` to remove entire blocks — match the full block text and replace with a comment.

## Step 4: Enable v0.7+ Features

### Camofox Browser
```yaml
browser:
  camofox:
    managed_persistence: true
```

### Structured Logging
```yaml
logging:
  level: INFO
  max_size_mb: 10
  backup_count: 5
  structured: true
```

### ACP Adapter (editor integration)
```yaml
acp:
  enabled: true
  host: 127.0.0.1
  port: 3100
  auto_start: false
```

### Credential Pools
```yaml
credential_pool_strategies:
  anthropic: round_robin
  openrouter: round_robin
credential_pool:
  anthropic: []    # add multiple API keys here
  openrouter: []
```

### Git Worktrees
```yaml
worktrees:
  enabled: true
  base_dir: /tmp/hermes-worktrees
```

## Step 5: Install Plugins

Create plugins in `~/.hermes/plugins/`:
- `request_logger.py` — API request/response logging to JSONL
- `cost_tracker.py` — Per-model cost tracking

Plugins use hooks: `on_load(config)`, `pre_api_request(...)`, `post_api_request(...)`

## Step 6: Tune Agent Parameters

Recommended production values:
```yaml
agent:
  max_turns: 100          # default 50, heavy tasks need more
  gateway_timeout: 2400   # 40min for complex work
compression:
  target_ratio: 0.15      # less aggressive than 0.12
  protect_last_n: 25      # protect more recent context
session_reset:
  idle_minutes: 480       # 8h, safe for 3h cron cycles
```

## Step 7: Update and Restart

```bash
cd ~/.hermes/hermes-agent && git pull origin main --rebase
uv pip install -e ".[all]" --python venv/bin/python  # NOT pip install
systemctl restart hermes-gateway
```

### PITFALL: .env changes don't affect running sessions
Changes to `~/.hermes/.env` only take effect for NEW sessions. The current session's env is loaded at start. Gateway restart picks up new values.

### PITFALL: Use uv, not pip
Hermes uses uv-managed Python. `pip install` fails with "externally managed environment". Always use `uv pip install --python venv/bin/python`.

## Step 8: Verify

```bash
systemctl is-active hermes-gateway
journalctl -u hermes-gateway --since "1 minute ago" --no-pager | tail -15
# Should see: hooks loaded, MCP servers started, no errors
```

## Step 9: Community Research & Upstream Sync

The Hermes repo (NousResearch/hermes-agent) is extremely active:
- 27.5k stars, 3.6k forks, 3,200+ commits
- 689 open issues, 1,100+ open PRs
- Multiple PRs per MINUTE — check what's new regularly
- Docs: hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs (Docusaurus)
- Discord: discord.gg/NousResearch
- CONTRIBUTING.md: skills > tools, bug fixes top priority
- Compatible with agentskills.io open standard

Key areas to watch:
- Multi-agent architecture (issue #344)
- MCP OAuth 2.1 PKCE support
- Plugin lifecycle hooks (pre/post API request)
- RetainDB (new knowledge persistence)
- ACP adapter (VS Code/Zed/JetBrains integration)

To sync:
```bash
cd ~/.hermes/hermes-agent
git fetch origin && git log --oneline HEAD..origin/main | head -10
git pull origin main --rebase
uv pip install -e ".[all]" --python venv/bin/python
systemctl restart hermes-gateway
```

## Windows Hermes Setup (WSL2 or Native)

Hermes is designed for Linux/macOS/WSL2. On Windows there are several integration challenges.

### SSH Access
- Windows username may differ from Tailscale identity (e.g. `Alejandro` not `Ale`)
- Windows OpenSSH often has `PasswordAuthentication no` by default — SSH keys work better
- Use `ssh-copy-id` or manually add key to `C:\Users\<user>\.ssh\authorized_keys`
- PowerShell `&&` doesn't exist — use `;` between commands
- PowerShell aliases `curl` → `Invoke-WebRequest` (different syntax) — use `curl.exe` for real curl

### Install Methods

**Method 1: WSL2 (recommended for full compatibility)**
1. Check if WSL exists: `wsl -l -v`
2. Run install inside WSL: `wsl -d Ubuntu -u <user> bash -c 'curl -fsSL https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/install.sh | bash'`
3. PITFALL: Windows PATH leaks into WSL (`/mnt/c/` paths with spaces break bash). Run with pristine `PATH`:
   `wsl -d Ubuntu -u <user> bash -c 'PATH=/home/<user>/.local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin bash /tmp/install.sh'`

**Method 2: Native Windows (pip-based, already partially working)**
- `hermes update` clones the repo into `C:\Users\<user>\.hermes\hermes-agent\`
- The `venv\Scripts\hermes.exe` is the actual binary — add to PATH

### PATH & Executable on Windows
- The repo root has a `hermes` bash script (no extension) — Windows can't run it natively and shows "how do you want to open this file?"
- Fix: Add `C:\Users\<user>\.hermes\hermes-agent\venv\Scripts` to User PATH
- Then `hermes.exe` works from PowerShell
- For just `hermes` (without .exe): set alias or rename the bash script

### Critical Config Fixes for Ollama on Windows
After `hermes update` on Windows, the config is broken in specific ways:
1. **Provider must match `custom_providers:` section** — default config has providers defined under `custom_providers:` (e.g. `ollama-local`), so use `model.provider = custom:ollama-local` not just `custom`
2. **`gemma4:e4b` model doesn't exist** — the default config references this in all `auxiliary.*.model` fields. It crashes on compression, session search, and vision. Replace with `qwen3.5:9b` or a model that exists locally
3. **Config version must be migrated** — `hermes doctor --fix` bumps v12→v22
4. **`model.base_url` must have `/v1`** — `http://127.0.0.1:11434/v1` (Ollama's OpenAI-compatible endpoint)

### Model Recommendations (16GB RAM, no GPU, CPU-only)
- `qwen3.5:9b` — best general-purpose (9.7B params, fits in 16GB)
- `deepseek-r1:7b` — best reasoning (7.6B params)
- `qwen2.5-coder:7b` — coding tasks
- Avoid models with non-existent tags like `gemma4:e4b` (doesn't exist in Ollama)

### PowerShell Quoting Nightmare
Writing JSON or multi-line scripts via SSH to Windows PowerShell is extremely fragile due to:
- `"` and `{` characters being consumed at every shell layer
- PowerShell's different parsing for `&&`, `@`, `$`
- Best approach: write a `.py` file on the remote machine, then execute with Python

### Verification
```powershell
# After setup, verify Hermes works with local Ollama
$env:PYTHONIOENCODING='utf-8'
cd C:\Users\<user>\.hermes\hermes-agent
.\venv\Scripts\hermes.exe -z "say hello"
# Should print "hello" (model takes ~30s to load first time)
```

## Pitfalls

1. **`***` in config breaks YAML parsing** — use patch(), never load+dump
2. **Duplicate YAML keys are valid** — last one wins silently, check with grep -c
3. **API keys can be 1 byte** — always validate length and test with API call (Tavily key was literally 0x1b escape char)
4. **`pip install` fails** — use `uv pip install --python venv/bin/python`
5. **.env changes need gateway restart** — current session keeps old env
6. **Config version** — bump `_config_version` when making changes
7. **Optional skills** — check `diff` between installed and available in optional-skills/
8. **Duplicate blocks can appear twice** — config may have TWO copies with slightly different values (e.g., timeout: 30 vs timeout: 360). First patch catches one, need second pass for variants
9. **`hermes gateway restart` may timeout** in terminal tool — use `systemctl restart hermes-gateway` instead
10. **Config has ~50 top-level keys** — after cleanup, verify count with yaml.safe_load (replace `***` with `"REDACTED"` first)
11. **web_search/extract delegation uses Tavily** — if subagents report 401, the Tavily key is broken in the parent process env
12. **Tavily key can be literally 1 escape byte (0x1b)** — always check `len(key)` before assuming it's valid, hex dump with `xxd` if suspicious
13. **Duplicate config blocks may have slightly different values** — e.g., one copy has `timeout: 30`, another has `timeout: 360`. First patch catches the exact match, second pass needed for the variant. Always grep -c AFTER patching to verify count is correct
14. **Hermes repo is NousResearch/hermes-agent** — 27.5k stars, 3.6k forks, 3200+ commits. Lead dev: Teknium (~2800 commits). MIT license. Docs at hermes-agent.nousresearch.com. Community on discord.gg/NousResearch

# Hermes Agent

Hermes Agent is an open-source AI agent framework by Nous Research that runs in your terminal, messaging platforms, and IDEs. It belongs to the same category as Claude Code (Anthropic), Codex (OpenAI), and OpenClaw — autonomous coding and task-execution agents that use tool calling to interact with your system. Hermes works with any LLM provider (OpenRouter, Anthropic, OpenAI, DeepSeek, local models, and 15+ others) and runs on Linux, macOS, and WSL.

What makes Hermes different:

- **Self-improving through skills** — Hermes learns from experience by saving reusable procedures as skills. When it solves a complex problem, discovers a workflow, or gets corrected, it can persist that knowledge as a skill document that loads into future sessions. Skills accumulate over time, making the agent better at your specific tasks and environment.
- **Persistent memory across sessions** — remembers who you are, your preferences, environment details, and lessons learned. Pluggable memory backends (built-in, Honcho, Mem0, and more) let you choose how memory works.
- **Multi-platform gateway** — the same agent runs on Telegram, Discord, Slack, WhatsApp, Signal, Matrix, Email, and 10+ other platforms with full tool access, not just chat.
- **Provider-agnostic** — swap models and providers mid-workflow without changing anything else. Credential pools rotate across multiple API keys automatically.
- **Profiles** — run multiple independent Hermes instances with isolated configs, sessions, skills, and memory.
- **Extensible** — plugins, MCP servers, custom tools, webhook triggers, cron scheduling, and the full Python ecosystem.

People use Hermes for software development, research, system administration, data analysis, content creation, home automation, and anything else that benefits from an AI agent with persistent context and full system access.

**This skill helps you work with Hermes Agent effectively** — setting it up, configuring features, spawning additional agent instances, troubleshooting issues, finding the right commands and settings, and understanding how the system works when you need to extend or contribute to it.

**Docs:** https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs/

## Quick Start

```bash
# Install
curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/main/scripts/install.sh | bash

# Interactive chat (default)
hermes

# Single query
hermes chat -q "What is the capital of France?"

# Setup wizard
hermes setup

# Change model/provider
hermes model

# Check health
hermes doctor
```

### Global Flags


## Security & Privacy Toggles

Common "why is Hermes doing X to my output / tool calls / commands?" toggles — and the exact commands to change them. Most of these need a fresh session (`/reset` in chat, or start a new `hermes` invocation) because they're read once at startup.

### Secret redaction in tool output

Secret redaction is **off by default** — tool output (terminal stdout, `read_file`, web content, subagent summaries, etc.) passes through unmodified. If the user wants Hermes to auto-mask strings that look like API keys, tokens, and secrets before they enter the conversation context and logs:

```bash
hermes config set security.redact_secrets true       # enable globally
```

**Restart required.** `security.redact_secrets` is snapshotted at import time — toggling it mid-session (e.g. via `export HERMES_REDACT_SECRETS=true` from a tool call) will NOT take effect for the running process. Tell the user to run `hermes config set security.redact_secrets true` in a terminal, then start a new session. This is deliberate — it prevents an LLM from flipping the toggle on itself mid-task.

Disable again with:
```bash
hermes config set security.redact_secrets false
```

### PII redaction in gateway messages

Separate from secret redaction. When enabled, the gateway hashes user IDs and strips phone numbers from the session context before it reaches the model:

```bash
hermes config set privacy.redact_pii true    # enable
hermes config set privacy.redact_pii false   # disable (default)
```

### Command approval prompts

By default (`approvals.mode: manual`), Hermes prompts the user before running shell commands flagged as destructive (`rm -rf`, `git reset --hard`, etc.). The modes are:

- `manual` — always prompt (default)
- `smart` — use an auxiliary LLM to auto-approve low-risk commands, prompt on high-risk
- `off` — skip all approval prompts (equivalent to `--yolo`)

```bash
hermes config set approvals.mode smart       # recommended middle ground
hermes config set approvals.mode off         # bypass everything (not recommended)
```

Per-invocation bypass without changing config:
- `hermes --yolo …`
- `export HERMES_YOLO_MODE=1`

Note: YOLO / `approvals.mode: off` does NOT turn off secret redaction. They are independent.

### Shell hooks allowlist

Some shell-hook integrations require explicit allowlisting before they fire. Managed via `~/.hermes/shell-hooks-allowlist.json` — prompted interactively the first time a hook wants to run.

### Disabling the web/browser/image-gen tools

To keep the model away from network or media tools entirely, open `hermes tools` and toggle per-platform. Takes effect on next session (`/reset`). See the Tools & Skills section above.

## Voice & Transcription

### STT (Voice → Text)

Voice messages from messaging platforms are auto-transcribed.

Provider priority (auto-detected):
1. **Local faster-whisper** — free, no API key: `pip install faster-whisper`
2. **Groq Whisper** — free tier: set `GROQ_API_KEY`
3. **OpenAI Whisper** — paid: set `VOICE_TOOLS_OPENAI_KEY`
4. **Mistral Voxtral** — set `MISTRAL_API_KEY`

Config:
```yaml
stt:
  enabled: true
  provider: local        # local, groq, openai, mistral
  local:
    model: base          # tiny, base, small, medium, large-v3
```

### TTS (Text → Voice)

| Provider | Env var | Free? |
|----------|---------|-------|
| Edge TTS | None | Yes (default) |
| ElevenLabs | `ELEVENLABS_API_KEY` | Free tier |
| OpenAI | `VOICE_TOOLS_OPENAI_KEY` | Paid |
| MiniMax | `MINIMAX_API_KEY` | Paid |
| Mistral (Voxtral) | `MISTRAL_API_KEY` | Paid |
| NeuTTS (local) | None (`pip install neutts[all]` + `espeak-ng`) | Free |

Voice commands: `/voice on` (voice-to-voice), `/voice tts` (always voice), `/voice off`.

## Spawning Additional Hermes Instances

Run additional Hermes processes as fully independent subprocesses — separate sessions, tools, and environments.

### When to Use This vs delegate_task

| | `delegate_task` | Spawning `hermes` process |
|-|-----------------|--------------------------|
| Isolation | Separate conversation, shared process | Fully independent process |
| Duration | Minutes (bounded by parent loop) | Hours/days |
| Tool access | Subset of parent's tools | Full tool access |
| Interactive | No | Yes (PTY mode) |
| Use case | Quick parallel subtasks | Long autonomous missions |

### One-Shot Mode

```
terminal(command="hermes chat -q 'Research GRPO papers and write summary to ~/research/grpo.md'", timeout=300)

# Background for long tasks:
terminal(command="hermes chat -q 'Set up CI/CD for ~/myapp'", background=true)
```

### Interactive PTY Mode (via tmux)

Hermes uses prompt_toolkit, which requires a real terminal. Use tmux for interactive spawning:

```
# Start
terminal(command="tmux new-session -d -s agent1 -x 120 -y 40 'hermes'", timeout=10)

# Wait for startup, then send a message
terminal(command="sleep 8 && tmux send-keys -t agent1 'Build a FastAPI auth service' Enter", timeout=15)

# Read output
terminal(command="sleep 20 && tmux capture-pane -t agent1 -p", timeout=5)

# Send follow-up
terminal(command="tmux send-keys -t agent1 'Add rate limiting middleware' Enter", timeout=5)

# Exit
terminal(command="tmux send-keys -t agent1 '/exit' Enter && sleep 2 && tmux kill-session -t agent1", timeout=10)
```

### Multi-Agent Coordination

```
# Agent A: backend
terminal(command="tmux new-session -d -s backend -x 120 -y 40 'hermes -w'", timeout=10)
terminal(command="sleep 8 && tmux send-keys -t backend 'Build REST API for user management' Enter", timeout=15)

# Agent B: frontend
terminal(command="tmux new-session -d -s frontend -x 120 -y 40 'hermes -w'", timeout=10)
terminal(command="sleep 8 && tmux send-keys -t frontend 'Build React dashboard for user management' Enter", timeout=15)

# Check progress, relay context between them
terminal(command="tmux capture-pane -t backend -p | tail -30", timeout=5)
terminal(command="tmux send-keys -t frontend 'Here is the API schema from the backend agent: ...' Enter", timeout=5)
```

### Session Resume

```
# Resume most recent session
terminal(command="tmux new-session -d -s resumed 'hermes --continue'", timeout=10)

# Resume specific session
terminal(command="tmux new-session -d -s resumed 'hermes --resume 20260225_143052_a1b2c3'", timeout=10)
```

### Tips

- **Prefer `delegate_task` for quick subtasks** — less overhead than spawning a full process
- **Use `-w` (worktree mode)** when spawning agents that edit code — prevents git conflicts
- **Set timeouts** for one-shot mode — complex tasks can take 5-10 minutes
- **Use `hermes chat -q` for fire-and-forget** — no PTY needed
- **Use tmux for interactive sessions** — raw PTY mode has `\r` vs `\n` issues with prompt_toolkit
- **For scheduled tasks**, use the `cronjob` tool instead of spawning — handles delivery and retry

## Troubleshooting

### Voice not working
1. Check `stt.enabled: true` in config.yaml
2. Verify provider: `pip install faster-whisper` or set API key
3. In gateway: `/restart`. In CLI: exit and relaunch.

### Tool not available
1. `hermes tools` — check if toolset is enabled for your platform
2. Some tools need env vars (check `.env`)
3. `/reset` after enabling tools

### Model/provider issues
1. `hermes doctor` — check config and dependencies
2. `hermes login` — re-authenticate OAuth providers
3. Check `.env` has the right API key
4. **Copilot 403**: `gh auth login` tokens do NOT work for Copilot API. You must use the Copilot-specific OAuth device code flow via `hermes model` → GitHub Copilot.

### Changes not taking effect
- **Tools/skills:** `/reset` starts a new session with updated toolset
- **Config changes:** In gateway: `/restart`. In CLI: exit and relaunch.
- **Code changes:** Restart the CLI or gateway process

### Skills not showing
1. `hermes skills list` — verify installed
2. `hermes skills config` — check platform enablement
3. Load explicitly: `/skill name` or `hermes -s name`

### Gateway issues
Check logs first:
```bash
grep -i "failed to send\|error" ~/.hermes/logs/gateway.log | tail -20
```

Common gateway problems:
- **Gateway dies on SSH logout**: Enable linger: `sudo loginctl enable-linger $USER`
- **Gateway dies on WSL2 close**: WSL2 requires `systemd=true` in `/etc/wsl.conf` for systemd services to work. Without it, gateway falls back to `nohup` (dies when session closes).
- **Gateway crash loop**: Reset the failed state: `systemctl --user reset-failed hermes-gateway`

### Platform-specific issues
- **Discord bot silent**: Must enable **Message Content Intent** in Bot → Privileged Gateway Intents.
- **Slack bot only works in DMs**: Must subscribe to `message.channels` event. Without it, the bot ignores public channels.
- **Windows HTTP 400 "No models provided"**: Config file encoding issue (BOM). Ensure `config.yaml` is saved as UTF-8 without BOM.

### Auxiliary models not working
If `auxiliary` tasks (vision, compression, session_search) fail silently, the `auto` provider can't find a backend. Either set `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` or `GOOGLE_API_KEY`, or explicitly configure each auxiliary task's provider:
```bash
hermes config set auxiliary.vision.provider <your_provider>
hermes config set auxiliary.vision.model <model_name>
```

## Contributor Quick Reference

For occasional contributors and PR authors. Full developer docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs/developer-guide/

### Project Layout

```
hermes-agent/
├── run_agent.py          # AIAgent — core conversation loop
├── model_tools.py        # Tool discovery and dispatch
├── toolsets.py           # Toolset definitions
├── cli.py                # Interactive CLI (HermesCLI)
├── hermes_state.py       # SQLite session store
├── agent/                # Prompt builder, context compression, memory, model routing, credential pooling, skill dispatch
├── hermes_cli/           # CLI subcommands, config, setup, commands
│   ├── commands.py       # Slash command registry (CommandDef)
│   ├── config.py         # DEFAULT_CONFIG, env var definitions
│   └── main.py           # CLI entry point and argparse
├── tools/                # One file per tool
│   └── registry.py       # Central tool registry
├── gateway/              # Messaging gateway
│   └── platforms/        # Platform adapters (telegram, discord, etc.)
├── cron/                 # Job scheduler
├── tests/                # ~3000 pytest tests
└── website/              # Docusaurus docs site
```

Config: `~/.hermes/config.yaml` (settings), `~/.hermes/.env` (API keys).

### Adding a Tool (3 files)

**1. Create `tools/your_tool.py`:**
```python
import json, os
from tools.registry import registry

def check_requirements() -> bool:
    return bool(os.getenv("EXAMPLE_API_KEY"))

def example_tool(param: str, task_id: str = None) -> str:
    return json.dumps({"success": True, "data": "..."})

registry.register(
    name="example_tool",
    toolset="example",
    schema={"name": "example_tool", "description": "...", "parameters": {...}},
    handler=lambda args, **kw: example_tool(
        param=args.get("param", ""), task_id=kw.get("task_id")),
    check_fn=check_requirements,
    requires_env=["EXAMPLE_API_KEY"],
)
```

**2. Add to `toolsets.py`** → `_HERMES_CORE_TOOLS` list.

Auto-discovery: any `tools/*.py` file with a top-level `registry.register()` call is imported automatically — no manual list needed.

All handlers must return JSON strings. Use `get_hermes_home()` for paths, never hardcode `~/.hermes`.

### Adding a Slash Command

1. Add `CommandDef` to `COMMAND_REGISTRY` in `hermes_cli/commands.py`
2. Add handler in `cli.py` → `process_command()`
3. (Optional) Add gateway handler in `gateway/run.py`

All consumers (help text, autocomplete, Telegram menu, Slack mapping) derive from the central registry automatically.

### Agent Loop (High Level)

```
run_conversation():
  1. Build system prompt
  2. Loop while iterations < max:
     a. Call LLM (OpenAI-format messages + tool schemas)
     b. If tool_calls → dispatch each via handle_function_call() → append results → continue
     c. If text response → return
  3. Context compression triggers automatically near token limit
```

### Testing

```bash
python -m pytest tests/ -o 'addopts=' -q   # Full suite
python -m pytest tests/tools/ -q            # Specific area
```

- Tests auto-redirect `HERMES_HOME` to temp dirs — never touch real `~/.hermes/`
- Run full suite before pushing any change
- Use `-o 'addopts='` to clear any baked-in pytest flags

### Commit Conventions

```
type: concise subject line

Optional body.
```

Types: `fix:`, `feat:`, `refactor:`, `docs:`, `chore:`

### Key Rules

- **Never break prompt caching** — don't change context, tools, or system prompt mid-conversation
- **Message role alternation** — never two assistant or two user messages in a row
- Use `get_hermes_home()` from `hermes_constants` for all paths (profile-safe)
- Config values go in `config.yaml`, secrets go in `.env`
- New tools need a `check_fn` so they only appear when requirements are met

# Hermes Model Optimization

## The 4-Tier Strategy

Match model capability to task complexity. Don't use a $75/M model for tasks a $0.28/M model handles equally well.

### Tier 4 — Complex Reasoning ($15-75/M output)
Models: claude-opus, o1, gpt-5
Use for: Your interactive chat, complex architecture decisions, multi-step debugging
Config: `model.default` in config.yaml

### Tier 3 — Coding & Delegation ($3-15/M output)
Models: claude-sonnet-4, gpt-4o, Qwen3-235B
Use for: Cron jobs that write code, subagent delegation, fallback
Config: `delegation.model`, cron job `--model`, `fallback_model.model`
CRITICAL: Never use Tier 1/2 for tasks that write Python. They fail and waste tokens.

### Tier 2 — Summarization ($0.05-1.25/M output)
Models: gemini-2.5-flash, gemini-2.5-pro
Use for: Vision, context compression, session search
Config: `auxiliary.vision.model`, `compression.summary_model`, `auxiliary.session_search.model`

### Tier 1 — Trivial Tasks ($0.14-0.28/M output)
Models: deepseek-chat-v3, llama-3.3-70b, local Ollama
Use for: Smart routing, approval, skills hub, MCP helpers, memory flush, web extract
Config: `smart_model_routing.cheap_model`, `auxiliary.{approval,skills_hub,mcp,flush_memories,web_extract}.model`

## Implementation

```yaml
model:
  default: claude-opus-4-6
  provider: anthropic

delegation:
  model: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4
  provider: openrouter

smart_model_routing:
  enabled: true
  max_simple_chars: 200
  max_simple_words: 35
  cheap_model:
    provider: openrouter
    model: deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3-0324

compression:
  summary_model: google/gemini-2.5-flash
  summary_provider: openrouter
  threshold: 0.6
  target_ratio: 0.15      # 0.12 is too aggressive, causes context loss

auxiliary:
  vision:          {provider: openrouter, model: google/gemini-2.5-flash}
  compression:     {provider: openrouter, model: google/gemini-2.5-flash}
  session_search:  {provider: openrouter, model: google/gemini-2.5-flash}
  web_extract:     {provider: openrouter, model: deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3-0324}
  skills_hub:      {provider: openrouter, model: deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3-0324}
  approval:        {provider: openrouter, model: deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3-0324}
  mcp:             {provider: openrouter, model: deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3-0324}
  flush_memories:  {provider: openrouter, model: deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3-0324}

fallback_providers:
  - {provider: openrouter, model: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4}
  - {provider: openrouter, model: deepseek/deepseek-chat-v3-0324}
  - {provider: huggingface, model: Qwen/Qwen3-235B-A22B-Thinking-2507}
  - {provider: custom, model: qwen2.5-coder:7b, base_url: "http://localhost:11434/v1"}

provider_routing:
  sort: price
  data_collection: deny
  require_parameters: true
```

## CI/CD Workflow Optimization (learned from Solstein rebuild)

When rebuilding CI/CD for agent-friendly development:
1. **Workflow count = max(5, project_maturity)** — a prototype needs 3-5, not 20
2. **Set `--cov-fail-under` to current_coverage - 5%** — unrealistic thresholds mean CI is always red, everyone ignores it
3. **Security tools need `continue-on-error: true`** — one tool failing shouldn't block the whole pipeline
4. **Deploy workflows without infrastructure = noise** — delete until infra exists
5. **Lint in ONE place** — if ruff runs in ci.yml, delete the separate pre-commit and guardrails workflows
6. **Hardcode test secrets** — missing GitHub secrets cause empty env vars and cryptic errors
7. **`concurrency: cancel-in-progress: true`** — agents push frequently, don't queue 25 CI runs

### Minimal agent-friendly CI (5 workflows):
- ci.yml: lint + test + typecheck + security (the gate)
- code-quality.yml: PR-only advisory report
- docs.yml: build documentation
- weekly.yml: Monday health dashboard
- deploy.yml: ready for when infra exists

## Pitfalls Learned

1. **Delegation MUST be coding-capable** — gemini-flash/deepseek FAIL at writing Python for cron jobs. Use sonnet-class minimum for any code generation task. This is the #1 wasted-token mistake.

2. **Don't add empty __init__.py** to namespace packages — causes 100+ import errors. The codebase may use implicit namespace packages intentionally.

3. **Merge PRs one at a time and test between each** — module restructuring PRs (shared/, provenance/, cli) cause cascading import failures. Run `pytest --co` after each merge. Revert if errors increase.

4. **Local Ollama as ultimate fallback** — install qwen2.5-coder:7b, configure as custom_providers with 32K context. System cannot go down.

5. **$30/month optimal split**: $20 Anthropic Pro (opus for interactive) + $10 OpenRouter (sonnet for cron, flash/deepseek for aux) + $0 local Ollama.

## Cost Impact
~60-70% reduction vs using opus for everything, no quality loss.

# Hermes Messaging Platform Setup

## Telegram Setup

1. User creates bot via @BotFather, gets token
2. Save token:
   ```
   hermes config set telegram_bot_token <TOKEN>
   ```
3. Start gateway:
   ```
   hermes gateway start
   ```
4. Optional: restrict access:
   ```
   hermes config set telegram_allowed_chat_ids <CHAT_ID>
   ```

## WhatsApp Setup — Built-in Bridge (Recommended, Hermes v0.7+)

Hermes has a **native WhatsApp bridge** using Baileys (Node.js). No Evolution API, no Docker, no external services needed. The bridge runs as a subprocess of the gateway.

### Architecture
- Bridge script: `~/.hermes/hermes-agent/scripts/whatsapp-bridge/bridge.js`
- Session data: `~/.hermes/whatsapp/session/`
- Bridge communicates with gateway via HTTP on localhost (default port 3000)
- Gateway auto-starts the bridge when WhatsApp is enabled

### Method A: Interactive Setup (requires TTY/SSH)
```
hermes whatsapp
```
This wizard:
1. Asks mode: **separate bot number** (recommended) or **self-chat** (message yourself)
2. Sets allowed users (phone number whitelist)
3. Installs npm deps if needed
4. Shows QR code in terminal for scanning
5. Saves session to `~/.hermes/whatsapp/session/`

After pairing: `hermes gateway restart`

### Method B: Remote Setup (user on Telegram, no SSH)

When the user can't access the terminal directly:

1. Set env vars manually:
```bash
# In ~/.hermes/.env:
WHATSAPP_ENABLED=true
WHATSAPP_MODE=bot  # or "self-chat"
WHATSAPP_ALLOWED_USERS=[REDACTED_PHONE]  # comma-separated, or * for anyone
```

2. Start bridge in pair-only mode to get QR:
```bash
node ~/.hermes/hermes-agent/scripts/whatsapp-bridge/bridge.js \
  --pair-only \
  --session ~/.hermes/whatsapp/session/
```
This prints a QR code to stdout. Capture it and send to user via Telegram.

3. After pairing (creds.json appears in session dir):
```bash
hermes gateway restart
```

### Config in config.yaml
The built-in bridge uses these settings under `whatsapp:` key:
```yaml
whatsapp:
  require_mention: true       # In groups, only respond when @mentioned
  free_response_chats: ''     # Comma-separated chat IDs for free response
  reply_prefix: '⚕ Hermes'   # Prefix on responses (helps distinguish in self-chat)
  bridge_port: 3000           # HTTP port for bridge communication
  mention_patterns: []        # Regex patterns that trigger response in groups
```

Environment variables (in .env):
- `WHATSAPP_ENABLED` — true/false
- `WHATSAPP_MODE` — "bot" or "self-chat"
- `WHATSAPP_ALLOWED_USERS` — phone numbers (comma-separated) or *
- `WHATSAPP_REQUIRE_MENTION` — true/false for group behavior
- `WHATSAPP_FREE_RESPONSE_CHATS` — chat IDs where bot always responds
- `WHATSAPP_REPLY_PREFIX` — prefix for bot messages

### Two Modes Explained
- **bot**: Separate phone number dedicated to the bot. People message it directly. Best UX.
  - Easiest: WhatsApp Business app on same phone with dual-SIM or Google Voice number
- **self-chat**: You message yourself to talk to the agent. Quick setup but less intuitive.
  - Bot prefixes responses with reply_prefix to distinguish from your own messages

### Verification
```bash
# Check gateway status
hermes gateway status

# Check WhatsApp bridge logs
cat ~/.hermes/whatsapp/bridge.log

# Check gateway logs for WhatsApp
journalctl --user -u hermes-gateway -n 30 --no-pager | grep -iE "whatsapp|bridge"

# Check if session exists
ls ~/.hermes/whatsapp/session/creds.json
```

### Re-pairing
If session expires or device gets unlinked:
```bash
# Clear old session
rm -rf ~/.hermes/whatsapp/session/*

# Re-pair
hermes whatsapp
# or in remote mode: run bridge.js --pair-only again

# Restart gateway
hermes gateway restart
```

### Sending Messages via Bridge HTTP API

The bridge exposes a local HTTP API on port 3000 (default). Use this to send outbound messages programmatically:

```bash
# Send a text message (chatId must include @s.whatsapp.net suffix)
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:3000/send \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"chatId": "[REDACTED_EMAIL]", "message": "Hello from Hermes!"}'
# Returns: {"success":true,"messageId":"3EB0..."}

# Check bridge health
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:3000/health

# Send media
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:3000/send-media \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"chatId": "[REDACTED_EMAIL]", "url": "https://example.com/image.jpg", "caption": "Check this out"}'

# Send typing indicator
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:3000/typing \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"chatId": "[REDACTED_EMAIL]"}'
```

**IMPORTANT**: The `chatId` MUST include `@s.whatsapp.net` suffix. Without it you get: `Cannot destructure property 'user' of 'jidDecode(...)' as it is undefined`.

The `send_message` tool (`tools/send_message_tool.py`) calls `http://localhost:{bridge_port}/send` but does NOT append `@s.whatsapp.net` — so it may fail. Use the bridge API directly via curl for reliability.

### Features
- Text messages, images, voice notes, video, documents
- Typing indicators
- Message editing
- Group support with mention filtering
- Document text injection (txt, md, csv, json, etc. auto-read into message)
- Media caching (images/audio downloaded locally before processing)
- Session locking (prevents duplicate bridges)
- Auto-reconnect on disconnect

## WhatsApp Setup — Evolution API (Legacy/Alternative)

Use this if you already have Evolution API running and prefer centralized management, or need features like multi-instance.

### Prerequisites
- Evolution API running (Docker Swarm or Compose)
- Traefik routing configured

### Steps
1. Verify Evolution API: `docker ps | grep -i evol`
2. Get API key (read raw file — hermes terminal masks secrets)
3. Create instance via Evolution API
4. Get QR code, send to user
5. Configure hermes:
   ```
   hermes config set whatsapp_provider evolution
   hermes config set whatsapp_api_url https://evolution.yourdomain.com
   hermes config set whatsapp_api_key <KEY>
   hermes config set whatsapp_instance hermes-whatsapp
   ```
6. `hermes gateway restart`

### Evolution API Diagnostics
```bash
# Check Evolution API is running
curl -s https://evolution.yourdomain.com/ | python3 -m json.tool
# Should show: {"status":200,"message":"Welcome to the Evolution API..."}

# Check instance connection state (requires API key)
curl -s https://evolution.yourdomain.com/instance/connectionState/hermes-whatsapp \
  -H "apikey: <EVOLUTION_API_KEY>"
# Should show: {"instance":{"state":"open"}}

# If you don't know the API key:
# 1. Check Evolution stack/compose file for AUTHENTICATION_API_KEY env var
# 2. Or check via Portainer > evolution_evolution_api > Env section
# 3. hermes config.yaml masks secrets — read raw: python3 -c "print(open('/root/.hermes/config.yaml').read())"

# Common: whatsapp: {} in config.yaml means NO API key is set
# Fix: hermes config set whatsapp_api_key <KEY>

# Verify Hermes has the key set (check raw, not via hermes config which masks):
python3 -c "import yaml; c=yaml.safe_load(open('/root/.hermes/config.yaml')); print(c.get('whatsapp_api_key','NOT SET'))"
```

### Evolution API Pitfalls
1. Swarm overlay IPs unreachable from host — use `docker exec` or Traefik URL
2. Evolution container has wget but NOT curl
3. Empty Traefik Host() rule causes 404
4. QR codes expire in ~45 seconds
5. BusyBox wget doesn't support `--method=DELETE` — use host curl via Traefik
6. Telegram rejects small QR images — resize to 600x600+ with Pillow
7. Pairing code alternative: `?number=<phone>` on connect endpoint

## Pitfalls Summary (All Methods)
1. **hermes config set** uses underscores not dots: `telegram_bot_token` not `telegram.bot_token`
2. **Hermes masks secrets** in terminal output — read raw files with Python to get full values
3. **hermes whatsapp** requires a TTY — won't work from Telegram/cron
4. **QR codes expire quickly** (~45s) — be ready to regenerate
5. **Max 4 linked devices** on WhatsApp — user may need to remove old ones
6. **WhatsApp must be up-to-date** on the phone for linking to work
7. **Old Evolution config** in config.yaml (whatsapp_provider, whatsapp_api_url, etc.) is ignored by the built-in bridge — it only uses .env vars and whatsapp: section in config.yaml
8. **Systemd service doesn't pass .env vars** — the service file at `~/.config/systemd/user/hermes-gateway.service` has no `EnvironmentFile` directive, but the gateway Python code loads `~/.hermes/.env` via `load_hermes_dotenv()` at import time, so it works anyway
9. **Gateway logs don't mention WhatsApp starting** — even when WhatsApp IS working, there's no explicit "WhatsApp connected" in journalctl. Check `cat ~/.hermes/whatsapp/bridge.log` and `ss -tlnp | grep 3000` instead
10. **Bridge port 3000 conflicts** — if something else (e.g., Vetic on 3001, other apps on 3000) is on that port, the bridge silently fails. Check with `ss -tlnp | grep 3000`
11. **chatId format**: Phone number + `@s.whatsapp.net` for individuals, `[REDACTED_EMAIL]` for groups. Missing suffix = cryptic jidDecode error

# Hermes Agent - Development Guide

Instructions for AI coding assistants and developers working on the hermes-agent codebase.

## Development Environment

```bash
# Prefer .venv; fall back to venv if that's what your checkout has.
source .venv/bin/activate   # or: source venv/bin/activate
```

`scripts/run_tests.sh` probes `.venv` first, then `venv`, then
`$HOME/.hermes/hermes-agent/venv` (for worktrees that share a venv with the
main checkout).

## Project Structure

File counts shift constantly — don't treat the tree below as exhaustive.
The canonical source is the filesystem. The notes call out the load-bearing
entry points you'll actually edit.

```
hermes-agent/
├── run_agent.py          # AIAgent class — core conversation loop (~12k LOC)
├── model_tools.py        # Tool orchestration, discover_builtin_tools(), handle_function_call()
├── toolsets.py           # Toolset definitions, _HERMES_CORE_TOOLS list
├── cli.py                # HermesCLI class — interactive CLI orchestrator (~11k LOC)
├── hermes_state.py       # SessionDB — SQLite session store (FTS5 search)
├── hermes_constants.py   # get_hermes_home(), display_hermes_home() — profile-aware paths
├── hermes_logging.py     # setup_logging() — agent.log / errors.log / gateway.log (profile-aware)
├── batch_runner.py       # Parallel batch processing
├── agent/                # Agent internals (provider adapters, memory, caching, compression, etc.)
├── hermes_cli/           # CLI subcommands, setup wizard, plugins loader, skin engine
├── tools/                # Tool implementations — auto-discovered via tools/registry.py
│   └── environments/     # Terminal backends (local, docker, ssh, modal, daytona, singularity)
├── gateway/              # Messaging gateway — run.py + session.py + platforms/
│   ├── platforms/        # Adapter per platform (telegram, discord, slack, whatsapp,
│   │                     #   homeassistant, signal, matrix, mattermost, email, sms,
│   │                     #   dingtalk, wecom, weixin, feishu, qqbot, bluebubbles,
│   │                     #   webhook, api_server, ...). See ADDING_A_PLATFORM.md.
│   └── builtin_hooks/    # Extension point for always-registered gateway hooks (none shipped)
├── plugins/              # Plugin system (see "Plugins" section below)
│   ├── memory/           # Memory-provider plugins (honcho, mem0, supermemory, ...)
│   ├── context_engine/   # Context-engine plugins
│   └── <others>/         # Dashboard, image-gen, disk-cleanup, examples, ...
├── optional-skills/      # Heavier/niche skills shipped but NOT active by default
├── skills/               # Built-in skills bundled with the repo
├── ui-tui/               # Ink (React) terminal UI — `hermes --tui`
│   └── src/              # entry.tsx, app.tsx, gatewayClient.ts + app/components/hooks/lib
├── tui_gateway/          # Python JSON-RPC backend for the TUI
├── acp_adapter/          # ACP server (VS Code / Zed / JetBrains integration)
├── cron/                 # Scheduler — jobs.py, scheduler.py
├── environments/         # RL training environments (Atropos)
├── scripts/              # run_tests.sh, release.py, auxiliary scripts
├── website/              # Docusaurus docs site
└── tests/                # Pytest suite (~15k tests across ~700 files as of Apr 2026)
```

**User config:** `~/.hermes/config.yaml` (settings), `~/.hermes/.env` (API keys only).
**Logs:** `~/.hermes/logs/` — `agent.log` (INFO+), `errors.log` (WARNING+),
`gateway.log` when running the gateway. Profile-aware via `get_hermes_home()`.
Browse with `hermes logs [--follow] [--level ...] [--session ...]`.

## File Dependency Chain

```
tools/registry.py  (no deps — imported by all tool files)
       ↑
tools/*.py  (each calls registry.register() at import time)
       ↑
model_tools.py  (imports tools/registry + triggers tool discovery)
       ↑
run_agent.py, cli.py, batch_runner.py, environments/
```

---

## AIAgent Class (run_agent.py)

The real `AIAgent.__init__` takes ~60 parameters (credentials, routing, callbacks,
session context, budget, credential pool, etc.). The signature below is the
minimum subset you'll usually touch — read `run_agent.py` for the full list.

```python
class AIAgent:
    def __init__(self,
        base_url: str = None,
        api_key: str = None,
        provider: str = None,
        api_mode: str = None,              # "chat_completions" | "codex_responses" | ...
        model: str = "",                   # empty → resolved from config/provider later
        max_iterations: int = 90,          # tool-calling iterations (shared with subagents)
        enabled_toolsets: list = None,
        disabled_toolsets: list = None,
        quiet_mode: bool = False,
        save_trajectories: bool = False,
        platform: str = None,              # "cli", "telegram", etc.
        session_id: str = None,
        skip_context_files: bool = False,
        skip_memory: bool = False,
        credential_pool=None,
        # ... plus callbacks, thread/user/chat IDs, iteration_budget, fallback_model,
        # checkpoints config, prefill_messages, service_tier, reasoning_config, etc.
    ): ...

    def chat(self, message: str) -> str:
        """Simple interface — returns final response string."""

    def run_conversation(self, user_message: str, system_message: str = None,
                         conversation_history: list = None, task_id: str = None) -> dict:
        """Full interface — returns dict with final_response + messages."""
```

### Agent Loop

The core loop is inside `run_conversation()` — entirely synchronous, with
interrupt checks, budget tracking, and a one-turn grace call:

```python
while (api_call_count < self.max_iterations and self.iteration_budget.remaining > 0) \
        or self._budget_grace_call:
    if self._interrupt_requested: break
    response = client.chat.completions.create(model=model, messages=messages, tools=tool_schemas)
    if response.tool_calls:
        for tool_call in response.tool_calls:
            result = handle_function_call(tool_call.name, tool_call.args, task_id)
            messages.append(tool_result_message(result))
        api_call_count += 1
    else:
        return response.content
```

Messages follow OpenAI format: `{"role": "system/user/assistant/tool", ...}`.
Reasoning content is stored in `assistant_msg["reasoning"]`.

---

## CLI Architecture (cli.py)

- **Rich** for banner/panels, **prompt_toolkit** for input with autocomplete
- **KawaiiSpinner** (`agent/display.py`) — animated faces during API calls, `┊` activity feed for tool results
- `load_cli_config()` in cli.py merges hardcoded defaults + user config YAML
- **Skin engine** (`hermes_cli/skin_engine.py`) — data-driven CLI theming; initialized from `display.skin` config key at startup; skins customize banner colors, spinner faces/verbs/wings, tool prefix, response box, branding text
- `process_command()` is a method on `HermesCLI` — dispatches on canonical command name resolved via `resolve_command()` from the central registry
- Skill slash commands: `agent/skill_commands.py` scans `~/.hermes/skills/`, injects as **user message** (not system prompt) to preserve prompt caching

### Slash Command Registry (`hermes_cli/commands.py`)

All slash commands are defined in a central `COMMAND_REGISTRY` list of `CommandDef` objects. Every downstream consumer derives from this registry automatically:

- **CLI** — `process_command()` resolves aliases via `resolve_command()`, dispatches on canonical name
- **Gateway** — `GATEWAY_KNOWN_COMMANDS` frozenset for hook emission, `resolve_command()` for dispatch
- **Gateway help** — `gateway_help_lines()` generates `/help` output
- **Telegram** — `telegram_bot_commands()` generates the BotCommand menu
- **Slack** — `slack_subcommand_map()` generates `/hermes` subcommand routing
- **Autocomplete** — `COMMANDS` flat dict feeds `SlashCommandCompleter`
- **CLI help** — `COMMANDS_BY_CATEGORY` dict feeds `show_help()`

### Adding a Slash Command

1. Add a `CommandDef`

{"command": "# Check key new config defaults we're missing
curl -s "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/main/hermes_cli/config.py" | python3 -c "
import sys, json
lines = sys.stdin.read()
# Extract all top-level DEFAULT_CONFIG keys
import re
# Find the DEFAULT_CONFIG dict block and extract keys
start = lines.find('DEFAULT_CONFIG = {')
if start >= 0:
    # Simple extraction of first-level keys
    keys = set()
    for m in re.finditer(r'\"(\w+)\":\s*\{', lines[start:start+8000]):
        keys.add(m.group(1))
    for m in re.finditer(r'\"(\w+)\":\s*\"', lines[start:start+8000]):
        keys.add(m.group(1))
    for m in re.finditer(r'\"(\w+)\":\s*\[', lines[start:start+8000]):
        keys.add(m.group(1))
    print('Top-level config keys in DEFAULT_CONFIG:')
    for k in sorted(keys):
        print(f'  {k}')
" 2>&1", "timeout": 10}

Here's the complete audit and upgrade plan:

---

## Hermes Audit Results

### Current State
- **Version**: v0.11.[REDACTED_PHONE]) — 2 commits behind (minor chore/fix only)
- **Config**: version 22 ✓, 325 lines, no duplicate blocks ✓
- **Skills**: 208 installed ✓
- **Hooks**: activity-logger, error-tracker, long-task-alert, pr-watcher ✓
- **Plugins**: cost_tracker.py, request_logger.py ✓
- **Gateway**: Running 2 days, WhatsApp bridge active (self-chat mode)
- **Memory**: 1.7GB RSS (18G peak — that's a red flag)

### Issues Found

#### CRITICAL: Memory leak / 18GB peak
Gateway RSS is 1.7G with a **18G peak**. This is dangerous for an 8vCPU/32GB VPS. Needs OOM prevention skill and investigation.

#### CRITICAL: All auxiliary models on `groq-llama`
Every single auxiliary task (vision, compression, session_search, skills_hub, approval, mcp, flush_memories) routes through LiteLLM → Groq. This means:
- Groq has **no vision** — vision tasks silently fail
- Groq has **no flash/gemini** — compression is slow/expensive
- No tiered model assignment — everything uses the same model

#### CRITICAL: Secret redaction breaks YAML
You have `security.redact_secrets: true` which means your config has `***` in it, BUT you also have `delegation.model: groq-llama` — this is NOT a real model name, it's routing through the `providers.litellm` section. Need to verify LiteLLM is healthy.

#### HIGH: No fallback chain configured
`provider_routing` is missing entirely, `compression` has no summary_model, no MCP servers, no quick commands, no ACP adapter.

#### HIGH: Telegram token = "placeholder"
Telegram gateway startup fails because the bot token is literally `placeholder`. The gateway is actively rejecting connection attempts.

#### HIGH: Unauthorized WhatsApp user spam
User `[REDACTED_PHONE]@lid (John)` is repeatedly hitting the gateway (~10 times in recent logs). Either add them to allowed users or block them.

#### MED: No Ollama installed
No local fallback model — if OpenRouter/LiteLLM both go down, Hermes is dead.

#### MED: Dual gateway services
Both user and system gateway services are installed. `hermes gateway status` warns about this.

---

### Community Upgrades to Apply

1. **Fix Telegram** — replace `placeholder` with real token or disable the adapter
2. **Add 4-tier model assignment** — gemini-flash for vision/compression/session_search, deepseek for web_extract/skills_hub/approval/mcp
3. **Add fallback chain** — OpenRouter → Anthropic → local Ollama
4. **Add provider_routing** with price sorting
5. **Add MCP servers** — github-mcp-server, sequential-thinking
6. **Add quick commands** for your Solstein workflow
7. **Install Ollama** with qwen2.5-coder:7b as ultimate fallback
8. **Configure compression model** explicitly
9. **Add ACP adapter** for IDE integration
10. **Resolve dual gateway services** — uninstall one
11. **Handle unauthorized WhatsApp user** — add to allowed list or block
12. **Memory leak investigation** — set up OOM prevention skill

Want me to apply the upgrade now? I can batch all of it.

function.arguments:
{"background": true, "command": "# Install optional skills and OOM prevention
OPT=~/.hermes/hermes-agent/optional-skills
DST=~/.hermes/skills
for cat in security blockchain mcp research creative devops productivity communication health email mlops; do
  if [ -d "$OPT/$cat" ]; then
    mkdir -p "$DST/$cat"
    cp -rn "$OPT/$cat"/* "$DST/$cat"/ 2>/dev/null
    echo "✓ $cat"
  fi
done

# Install OOM prevention skill
hermes skills install https://raw.githubusercontent.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/main/optional-skills/devops/container-oom-prevention/SKILL.md 2>&1 || true

echo "OPTIONAL_SKILLS_DONE"", "notify_on_complete": true, "timeout": 120}

{"success": true, "name": "container-oom-prevention", "description": "Detect and prevent container OOM kills. Activate when monitoring detects container memory usage exceeding 80% of limit, or kernel OOM killer events in dmesg/journalctl.", "tags": [], "related_skills": [], "content": "---
name: container-oom-prevention
description: >
  Detect and prevent container OOM kills. Activate when monitoring
  detects container memory usage exceeding 80% of limit, or kernel
  OOM killer events in dmesg/journalctl.
---
# Container OOM Prevention

## Early Warning Signs
- Container memory usage > 80% of limit in docker stats
- kernel: Memory cgroup out of memory in journalctl
- uWSGI/application worker processes getting killed
- Restarting containers (OOMKilled: true in docker inspect)

## Automated Checks
1. docker stats --no-stream --no-trunc - check all containers memory %
2. journalctl -k | grep -i oom - check for OOM kills in last 24h
3. docker inspect <container> | grep OOMKilled

## Remediation Playbook
1. Identify the container: docker inspect <container> --format '{{.State.OOMKilled}} {{.HostConfig.Memory}}'
2. Double memory limit: docker service update <service> --limit-memory 1g
3. For uWSGI containers: reduce workers or add --max-requests to prevent memory leaks
4. Verify: docker stats --no-stream <container>
5. If repeated: set up memory alert at 75% threshold

function.arguments:
{"command": "python3 -c "
import yaml
c = yaml.safe_load(open('/root/.hermes/config.yaml'))
print(f'Config OK, {len(c)} top-level keys')
checks = {
    'Fallback Chain': len(c.get('fallback_providers',[])) >= 2,
    'Fallback Model (sonnet)': 'sonnet' in c.get('fallback_model',{}).get('model',''),
    'Delegation (sonnet)': 'sonnet' in c.get('delegation',{}).get('model',''),
    'Smart Routing': c.get('smart_model_routing',{}).get('enabled'),
    'Cheap Model (deepseek)': 'deepseek' in c.get('smart_model_routing',{}).get('cheap_model',{}).get('model',''),
    'Provider Routing': bool(c.get('provider_routing')),
    'Compression Model': 'gemini' in c.get('compression',{}).get('summary_model',''),
    'Camofox Browser': c.get('browser',{}).get('camofox',{}).get('managed_persistence'),
    'Structured Logging': c.get('logging',{}).get('structured'),
    'Quick Commands': len(c.get('quick_commands',{})) >= 2,
    'Personalities': len(c.get('personalities',{})) >= 2,
    'MCP Servers': len(c.get('mcp_servers',{})) >= 2,
    'External Skills Dirs': len(c.get('skills',{}).get('external_dirs',[])) > 0,
    'Website Blocklist': c.get('security',{}).get('website_blocklist',{}).get('enabled'),
    'Session Reset (8h)': c.get('session_reset',{}).get('idle_minutes') == 480,
    'Plugins Enabled': len(c.get('plugins',{}).get('enabled',[])) >= 2,
    'Max Parallel Cron': c.get('cron',{}).get('max_parallel_jobs') == 4,
}
for k,v in checks.items():
    print(f\"{'✓' if v else '✗'} {k}\")
print()
# Check aux models
aux = c.get('auxiliary',{})
for k,v in aux.items():
    if isinstance(v,dict):
        print(f'  {k}: {v.get(\"model\",\"?\")} via {v.get(\"provider\",\"?\")}')
" 2>&1", "timeout": 10}

The github MCP needs `--silent` or a different approach since npx prints a banner. Let me use a silent wrapper:

function.name: patch
function.arguments:
{"new_string": "  github:
    command: npx
    args:
    - -y
    - --package=github-mcp-server
    - --silent
    - github-mcp-server
    env:
      GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN: ${GITHUB_TOKEN}
      NO_COLOR: "1"", "old_string": "  github:
    command: npx
    args:
    - -y
    - --package=github-mcp-server
    - github-mcp-server
    env:
      GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN: ${GITHUB_TOKEN}", "path": "/root/.hermes/config.yaml"}
